steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.amicos: 'Amicos' is a noun meaning 'friends' in the accusative
hints.iniquitatis: 'Iniquitatis' is a noun meaning 'of wickedness' in the genitive

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd pl. would be 'facitis' — 'Facite' is a command
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'faciatis' — 'Facite' is imperative
hints.infinitive: Infinitive would be 'facere' — 'Facite' is the plural imperative

hints.fallo, fallere, fefelli, falsum: These are the parts of 'fallere' (to deceive)
hints.fateor, fateri, fassus sum, —: These are the parts of 'fateri' (to confess) — a deponent
hints.fio, fieri, factus sum, —: These are the parts of 'fieri' (to become) — the passive of 'facere'

steps.reveal: vobis = for yourselves (dat., dative of advantage)

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'vos' — 'vobis' is dative here (for yourselves)
hints.ablative: 'Vobis' could be ablative, but here it expresses benefit — dative of advantage

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'amici' — 'amicos' with -os is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'amicis' — 'amicos' is accusative
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'amicis' — 'amicos' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'amici' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'amici' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'amici' ends in -i

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'iniquitas' — 'iniquitatis' with -is is genitive

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'iniquitatis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'iniquitatis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'iniquitatis' ends in -is

translation: Make friends for yourselves from the mammon of wickedness
